_meta.title: How to analyze an OSPF/IS-IS (Intermediate System to Intermediate System) topology offline
_meta.description: How to analyse the OSPF/IS-IS (Intermediate System to Intermediate System) topology offline / Find where a network is terminated, build the shortest paths, emulate links outage and get backup paths. Change OSPF/IS-IS edge cost on the fly and discover unsymmetric paths.
_meta.meta_keywords: topolograph, OSPF topology, OSPF visualization, OSPF BGP-LS topology, IS-IS BGP-LS topology, Intermediate System to Intermediate System visualization, IS-IS BGP-LS graph
_meta.og_title: How to analyze an OSPF/IS-IS topology offline
_meta.og_description: How to analyse the OSPF/IS-IS (Intermediate System to Intermediate System) topology offline / Find where a network is terminated, build the shortest paths, emulate links outage and get backup paths. Change OSPF/IS-IS edge cost on the fly and discover unsymmetric paths. BGP-LS for OSPF is supported since version 2.59.

p_ospfv3_intro: OSPFv3 (RFC 5340) LSDB topology visualization. Save the output from the command into a file with .txt or .log extension and upload it to Topolograph.
p_ospf_save_intro: Save the output from min two OSPF commands (for getting LSA1 and LSA2) or single IS-IS command and upload it to Topolograph with .txt or .log extension.
p_frr_isis_note: *FRR mixes LSPIDs and dynamic hostnames in IS-IS LSDB, that's why building IS-IS topology without hostnames is supported only. "no hostname dynamic" disable dynamic hostnames in local IS-IS LSDB.
p_gv_demo: Demo network has already uploaded for everyone. Press `Load dynamic graph` in order to upload it. Once you did it - you can see the topology, bold lines show ECMP links.
p_gv_ecmp: Press on bold line in order to unflat ECMP and see all nested links.
alt_gv_ecmp: Topolograph Press on bold line in order to unflat ECMP and see all nested links
h5_shortest_path: The shortest path. How to build.
p_spt_1: It's possible to build the shortest path from a node. Just right click on a node. The description with all nodes and path cost is available above the topology.
alt_spt_menu: Topolograph build the shortest path from a node
p_spt_2: Once you set the source and destination - you get colored the shortest path.
h5_all_shortest_paths: All the shortest paths to all nodes. Minimum Shortest Tree.
p_mst_1: New feature of v2.11 release. MST allows see all the shortest paths To or From node. Just activate it from this radio-checkbox.
alt_mst_menu: Topolograph MST allows see all the shortest paths To or From node
p_mst_incoming_pre: New feature of v2.11 release. All
p_mst_incoming_post: the shortest paths are built to the node.
p_mst_outgoing_pre: New feature of v2.11 release. All
p_mst_outgoing_post: the shortest paths are built from the node.
p_mst_diff_pre: New feature of v2.13 release. Difference between
p_mst_diff_post: the shortest paths of selected node.
alt_mst_diff: Topolograph difference between incoming and outgoing shortest paths
p_term_1: How is it possible to know what networks are terminated on some particular node. There are two ways how to know about it. The first one - start typing Node ID in "Find node by RID/IP. Find network" field and you will see all terminated networks on the node.
p_term_2: The second method is just to press on a node. New form pops up with a list of all backuped and non-backuped networks. The network is treated as backuped if such network is terminated on min two nodes.
p_backup: In order to find out the backup path - just press on colored edge. You will simulate a link outage and the shortest path will be rebuilt passing by a 'failed' edge.
h5_cost_on_fly: Shortest path rebuilding by changing ospf cost on the fly
p_cost_1: It's possible to change OSPF cost of any link. Right click on edge, new form pops up. Write new ospf cost in input field. Imagine if we need to redirect a traffic flow from 123.10.10.10 - 123.30.30.30 link to another link.
p_cost_2: Right click on colored edge and set 12. We get new traffic flow with new OSPF cost.
h5_compare_network: Compare state of your network in different time
p_compare: When you perform some configuration changes (add new network, redistribute another protocol to OSPF or change filters in existed route-maps) it is highly desired to make sure that results of your action give you expected changes in OSPF network. In order to do this - upload your network before your changes and right after it and compare them. What will be shown:
p_nrof_intro: In this mode it's possible to simulate a link or router shutdown/outage. The topology will be re-painted with expected changed traffic flow avoiding failed link or router.
h5_link_failure: Network reaction if a link/s goes down. Simulate link down.
p_link_failure_1_pre: Upload a graph if didn't do it before and press on
li_blue_lines: Blue lines show traffic increasing over the link
li_grey_lines: Grey lines show traffic decreasing over the link
p_link_failure_2: As you can see decreasing rate of traffic from 123.30.30.30 node to 123.123.30.30 and 123.123.31.31 differ from opposite direction [from 123.123.30.30 to 123.30.30.30] up to ~2 times. It happens because of the unsymmetric traffic path.
alt_link_failure_stat: Topolograph ospf link down simulation with stats
p_link_failure_3: We also can see an expected increasing rate of blue painted edge.
alt_link_failure_result: Topolograph ospf link down simulation result
h5_link_failure_dr_enabled: Network reaction if a link/s goes down on shared media.
p_dr_enabled: Topolograph builds a topology based on OSPF adjacency and physical connectivity is hidden. But in some cases, we can assume that neighbors share the same media, in some cases - not. For example, if neighbors have a common DR - we assume that they are connected via shared physical media and then we emulate one of the edge failures between such neighbors - we emulate all edges down.
p_dr_enabled_mark: Mark all shared media links (which have common DR) as enabled
alt_dr_enabled: Topolograph ospf link down simulation result with shared media
h5_link_failure_dr_disabled: Network reaction if a link/s goes down on dedicated media.
p_dr_disabled: If neighbors do not have DR or they have, but they are only two neighbors on the link, and when we emulate edge failure - we remove the only a single link between two particular neighbors.
p_dr_disabled_mark: Mark all shared media links (which have common DR) as disabled
alt_dr_disabled: Topolograph ospf link down simulation result with dedicated media
h5_node_failure: Network reaction if a network devices goes down. Simulate device down.
p_node_failure_1: Right click on a node and choose `shutdown this node`. The algorithm escapes this node.
alt_node_failure: Topolograph ospf Network reaction if a network devices goes down
p_node_failure_2: The network reaction on 123.123.101.101 node outage.
h5_cost_change: Network reaction on OSPF link cost changes.
p_cost_change_post: tab it is possible to see a network reaction on a OSPF cost change on the fly. Set new OSPF cost in right-clicked edge menu.
alt_cost_change: Topolograph ospf Network reaction on OSPF link cost changes
p_yaml_intro: OSPF/IS-IS LSDB <-> YAML is interchangeable now in both ways, so it allows to make a design of IGP domain from the scratch or based on uploaded LSDB, add new links/edges between nodes or change igp's cost and then check network reaction based on our changes.
p_yaml_node_name_mid: is mandatory. Should be in IP-address format. To change it to any other value - use
p_yaml_node_tags: Tags of node are optional. Any key (type string): value (str, int, float, dictionary, list) pairs.
alt_yaml_node_attr: Node attributes and REST API get nodes request
p_yaml_node_example_pre: There is a graph with 6 nodes. Select all primary nodes (
p_yaml_edge_cost_post: is optional. Default is 1. Equal to OSPF/IS-IS cost.
p_yaml_edge_tags: Tags of edge are optional. Any key (type string): value (str, int, float, dictionary, list) pairs.
alt_yaml_edge_attr: Edge attributes and REST API get edges request
h5_yaml_network_reaction: Network reaction on adding new link between devices.
p_yaml_reaction_pre: Let's add a new link with
p_yaml_reaction_post: 1 between R3 (10.10.10.3) and R4 (10.10.10.4) device and see how network will react on it.
alt_yaml_reaction: Topolograph network reaction on adding new link
p_yaml_reaction_result: Obviously, we see traffic increase on direct link R3<->R4 and traffic decrease to R2 (10.10.10.2) and R5 (10.10.10.5).
p_analytics_intro: In this mode it's possible to run algorithms in order to check your OSPF network.
p_uni_1: Print out any Unidirectional links (number of IN and OUT links between two neighbors is not equal)
alt_uni_menu: Topolograph ospf unidirectional and asymmetric links
p_uni_2: The edge is marked as red edge because of cost 1 and cost 10 OSPF metric
alt_uni_report: Topolograph ospf unidirectional and asymmetric links report
p_asym_1: Print out any asymmetric paths from different points of views.
p_asym_2: We get a list of nodes which have asymmetric paths. Just checking them - we can see difference in incoming and outgoing paths.
h5_ecmp: Main and backup paths via the same ECMP.
p_ecmp_1: We suggest that if we have multiple links bounded to ECMP and if the main link in ECMP goes down, the backup path should go via the second link in ECMP.
p_ecmp_2: If backup path goes not via ECMP and chooses completely different path - the report will be treated as failed.
alt_ecmp_failed: Topolograph ospf paths not via second link in ECMP
p_interarea_pre: This report checks that if two locations are directly connected, backup paths should be only between two locations and do not go via third location as transit. Before running this report - it's needed to create
p_interarea_example: For example, there are two locations in France: the main site (EU_FRA) and the remote site (EU_FRA1). The same schema with offices in Italy. The main offices in France and Italy are connected to each other and have main (OSPF cost 10) and backup (OSPF cost 20) link.
p_interarea_remote: Remote offices have the main link (OSPF cost 1) to their main offices and backup link (OSPF cost 10) to the foreign office.
p_interarea_failure: If the main link between main offices goes down - the backup path goes via remote offices!
alt_interarea_report: Topolograph ospf backup paths go via third party locations
p_heatmap_1_post: tab it is possible to see how many terminated subnets are backuped via terminating the same subnet on different devices.
p_heatmap_2: Click on a node in order to find out which networks are backuped which not.
alt_heatmap_networks: Topolograph ospf analytics network heatmap with networks
p_heatmap_3: As we can see only own device's loopback is not backuped - it is normal.
alt_heatmap_loopback: Topolograph ospf analytics network heatmap loopback
p_duplicated_quote: Analytics/Duplicated terminated networks. The issue with MPLS being affected by IP duplications in the network stems from the fact that labels are generated based on IP blocks, which can be duplicated. As a result, the same IP block may be associated with different labels. This leads to a situation where the same block, with different labels, is propagated to other routers, including the one with the duplicated IP. This becomes problematic when implementing MPLS Traffic Engineering (MPLS-TE) or Resource Reservation Protocol Traffic Engineering (RSVP-TE), as it can degrade performance and lead to routing bugs in relation to MPLS-TE, among other issues. Therefore, the issue of block duplication across links is critical in MPLS environments, primarily due to the generation of duplicate labels for the same block. In routers, this causes confusion when recalculating the path for TE or RSVP-TE.
p_duplicated_example: 10.0.0.0/24 network is terminated on four nodes: 172.16.1.2, 172.26.1.2 and 172.30.2.1, 178.20.3.1 are directly connected, but not between each other.
p_api_intro: You are free to choose your favorite NetDevOps tool like Ansible, netmiko, Nornir, Topolograph SDK, etc, and upload your OSPF network graph to Topolograph via a POST request. Once you uploaded the graph - you get the difference with previously uploaded graphs, specifically, you will receive:
p_api_intro_li1: new/old OSPF adjacencies (edges on the graph)
p_api_intro_li3: number of backuped and non backuped subnets in the topology
p_api_credentials: It's needed to create an account with email/password on Login/Registration page as well as add your source IP/Network to authorised source network list under API tab.
p_api_upload_intro: Save the output from commands describing OSPF LSA1, LSA2, LSA5 and save it in the single file - cisco_lsdb_output.txt
p_api_upload_python: Using python we read the content of the file and push it (it's better to say - POST it) to the topolograph.
h5_api_upload_multiple: Upload Multiple OSPF LSDBs via API
p_api_multi_1: Topology is built based on LSA1, LSA2, which are area only based. If a network has multiple areas, it is needed to save LSDB output from multiple devices and save it into separate files using the following format
p_api_multi_2_pre: For instance: there are two LSDBs
p_api_multi_3_post: folder, then wrapped into LSDB attribute dictionary
p_vrf_1: Multiple OSPF routing instances can exist on a single device in several VRFs. It causes nodes duplication on a visualisation graph. VRF tab allows to point multiple OSPF instances to a single one and avoid node duplication on a graph.
p_vrf_2: There are three VRFs and three OSPF instances on a router. In order to avoid node duplication - create VRFs (or import them via CSV), associate the node with VRF and point duplicated instances to master OSPF RID.
